title: Patchell–Spiro Conjecture (asymptotic optimality of CAT count)
statement_text: The construction giving $(3/2)n^2 - O(n)$ occurrences of the word CAT in an $n \times n$ grid (allowing horizontal, vertical, and diagonal occurrences) is asymptotically optimal; that is, the maximum number of CAT occurrences is $(3/2)n^2 + o(n^2)$.
context_text: Patchell and Spiro showed the number of CAT's in an $n\times n$ grid is at most $2n^2$, then constructed a labeling with $(3/2)n^2 - O(n)$ CAT's and conjectured it to be asymptotically optimal. The present paper confirms this in a precise form, proving the exact maximum is $(3^{d-1}/2)n^d$ on $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^d$ for all $d\geq 1$.
paper_contribution: Proves the exact tight bound $(3^{d-1}/2)n^d$ in all dimensions $d\geq 1$ and completely characterises equality cases as the $d\cdot 2^{2d-1+1}$ generalized Patchell–Spiro labelings (Theorem 1).
notes: The conjecture is stated informally in the paper's introduction without a labelled environment; the exact wording in [2] is not reproduced. PDF source — math may be garbled.

title: Patchell–Spiro Conjecture 1 (words with all distinct letters)
statement_text: For a word $w$ of length $r\geq 2$ in which all letters are distinct, the maximum number of occurrences of $w$ in a labeling of $(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^d$ is at most $\bigl(3^{d-1}/(r-1)\bigr)n^d$, with equality achieved by an appropriate generalization of the Patchell–Spiro labeling.
context_text: Patchell and Spiro asked what happens when CAT is replaced by another word, in particular words with all distinct letters such as LION and TIGER, and stated Conjecture 1 of [2] in this direction. The spectral argument used for CAT generalizes cleanly to this setting.
paper_contribution: Completely confirms Conjecture 1 of [2] via Theorem 3, establishing the tight bound $(3^{d-1}/(r-1))n^d$ for any word of length $r$ with all distinct letters in all dimensions $d$, with a full characterization of equality.
notes: The exact statement of Conjecture 1 from [2] is not reproduced in this paper; the statement is reconstructed from context. PDF source — math may be garbled.
